title: New Effective Results Regarding the Oppenheim Conjecture and Polynomial Effective Equidistribution
speaker: Elon Lindenstrauss
abstract:
Joint work with Amir Mohammadi, Zhiren Wang, and Lei Yang
Let Q be an indefinite ternary quadratic form. In the 1980s Margulis proved the longstanding Oppenheim Conjecture, stating that unless Q is proportional to an integral form, the set of values Q attains at the integer points is dense in R. We give quantitative results to that effect.
In particular, if the coefficients of Q are algebraic (but Q is not proportional to an integral form), and if (α,β)
 an interval not containing 0, the number of integer points v in a sphere of radius R for which α less than Q(v) less than β
 is asymptotic to the volume of the corresponding shape in R^3 with a power saving error term.
Our work is based on a new quantitative equidistribution result for unipotent flows, as well as upper bound estimates by Eskin-Margulis-Mozes and Wooyeon Kim.

proof_stages.label: Integer-value problem and positive-definite baseline
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the problem of understanding q(Z^d) for a real quadratic form and recalls the positive-definite lattice-point/gap picture, including the Davenport--Lewis-type expectation and circle-method counting heuristics.

proof_stages.label: Indefinite Oppenheim counting problem
proof_stages.summary: For indefinite forms the speaker contrasts rational and irrational behavior, recalls Oppenheim's density conjecture and Margulis's theorem for d>=3, reduces attention to ternary forms, and formulates the counting function N_Q(alpha,beta;t) together with its expected volume asymptotic.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic obstructions to naive asymptotics
proof_stages.summary: Examples with rational approximation, integral isotropic vectors, and degenerate rational subspaces show that irrationality alone cannot guarantee a clean asymptotic and that exceptional contributions must be isolated.

proof_stages.label: Diophantine condition and prior asymptotic results
proof_stages.summary: A projective Diophantine non-approximation condition for Q is introduced, algebraic forms are noted to satisfy such conditions, and earlier work of Dani--Margulis, Eskin--Margulis--Mozes, Kim, and others is positioned as giving lower bounds or asymptotics when the zero-value obstruction is controlled.

proof_stages.label: Effective asymptotic with explicit exceptional remainder
proof_stages.summary: The new joint theorem expresses N_Q(alpha,beta;t) as the expected volume plus a power-saving error and an explicit remainder supported on finitely many rational lines and planes, with an algebraic corollary after excluding zero vectors.

proof_stages.label: Homogeneous-dynamics reformulation
proof_stages.summary: Writing Q=Q_0 composed with g turns values of Q on Z^3 into values of a fixed model form on a unimodular lattice, and the large stabilizer H=SO(Q_0) lets Margulis's periodic-or-dense orbit dichotomy prove the qualitative Oppenheim theorem.

proof_stages.label: Effective expanding-unipotent equidistribution mechanism
proof_stages.summary: Shah/Ratner equidistribution of expanding unipotent averages represents the lattice count through an unbounded Siegel-transform-type function, and the new effective version plus non-divergence controls the error unless the arithmetic obstructions in the theorem occur.

examples.name: Weyl-law analogy for lattice counting
examples.note: An audience member compares the positive-definite lattice-point main term and error question with Weyl's law for Laplacian eigenvalues, serving only as an analogy for main-term/error phenomena.
examples.engagement_reason: the Weyl-law parallel is only invoked as an analogy for the leading asymptotic term, with no calculation or application carried out on it

examples.name: Binary indefinite form with isolated zero
examples.note: A binary indefinite quadratic form, related to the Markov spectrum, illustrates why the two-variable case behaves differently and can have zero isolated among values.
examples.engagement_reason: the binary indefinite form is given as an example where zero is isolated and linked to the Markov spectrum, but the speaker does not compute or verify the isolation in detail

passes.reason: The binary indefinite form is a nondegenerate ordinary form used to show that in dimension two zero may be isolated and Oppenheim-type density fails.
nondegenerate binary indefinite form with positive Markov minimum shows the dimension-two Oppenheim analogue can fail
a binary indefinite form with a positive minimum gap around zero is a critical two-variable boundary case refuting density-type behavior
A binary indefinite form whose zero value is isolated gives a boundary/gap case showing the Oppenheim density phenomenon cannot simply extend to dimension two.
A nondegenerate binary indefinite form with an isolated zero is used to show the two-variable analogue of Oppenheim-style density can fail.
a nondegenerate binary indefinite form with zero isolated in its integer value set is used to show the two-variable analogue can fail.
A binary indefinite form with 0 isolated is used to show the Oppenheim-type density phenomenon fails in dimension two, while the form itself is nondegenerate and not sporadic or maximally symmetric.
The binary indefinite form is invoked to show that the density/zero-accumulation phenomenon can fail in dimension two because zero may be isolated.
The binary indefinite form is invoked to show the Oppenheim-type density phenomenon fails in dimension two, the minimal indefinite dimension.
The binary indefinite form has a gap isolating 0, and is used to show the dimension-two analogue can fail.
The binary indefinite form is invoked to show the Oppenheim-type density phenomenon fails in dimension two because zero can be isolated.
The binary indefinite form is the minimal-dimensional indefinite case and is used to show zero can remain isolated in dimension two.

examples.name: Integral ternary cone with many zero values
examples.note: For a form such as 2xz-y^2 and the interval [-1/2,1/2], counting zero values via factorizations of y^2 gives too many lattice points and motivates excluding forms too well approximated by rational ones.
examples.engagement_reason: for the integral ternary cone the speaker counts zero solutions by choosing y up to sqrt(t) and then counting factorizations xz=y^2, deriving an excess of about t^{1/2}

passes.reason: The integral ternary form such as xz-y^2 is nondegenerate but is used to show the naive volume asymptotic can fail by having too many zero values.
split nondegenerate integral ternary form such as q=xz-y^2 has many integral zeros refuting the naive volume asymptotic without exclusions
the integral ternary form such as xz-y^2 is a nondegenerate rational example used to show the naive volume asymptotic can fail by excess zero values
The integral ternary form such as xz-y^2 is a nondegenerate rational split form used to show the naive volume asymptotic can fail by having too many zero values.
The split integral ternary form such as q=xz-y^2 is a nondegenerate rational family member whose many lattice zeros obstruct the naive volume asymptotic.
the split integral ternary form/cone has many lattice zeros and is used as an obstruction to the naive volume asymptotic without rationality exclusions.
The integral ternary form such as q=xz-y^2 supplies many zero values and obstructs the naive volume asymptotic, though the quadratic form is nondegenerate and not an exceptional object.
The integral split ternary cone such as xz-y^2=0 is an obstruction to the naive volume asymptotic by having maximally isotropic rational zero directions with too many integer zeros.
The integral ternary form such as the split cone q=xz-y^2 is used as an obstruction to the naive volume asymptotic because its zero set gives too many lattice points.
The integral split ternary form has a zero cone with an unusually large exact-zero count, refuting the naive volume asymptotic without exclusions.
The integral ternary form such as xz-y^2 is used as an obstruction to the naive volume asymptotic by producing too many zero values.
The integral split ternary form such as q=xz-y^2 has many integer zeros, obstructing the naive volume asymptotic.

examples.name: Irrational form with a rational isotropic line
examples.note: A form such as xz-sqrt(2)y^2 has an integral isotropic vector, so multiples of one rational line can contribute on the scale of the expected main term and force explicit line exceptions or removal of zero vectors.
examples.engagement_reason: for the irrational form with a rational isotropic vector, the speaker checks that a specific vector is isotropic and estimates the contribution of its multiples to the counting function

passes.reason: The irrational form is used as an obstruction to clean asymptotics because its rational isotropic line carries a collapsed zero restriction contributing many multiples.

The irrational form with a rational isotropic line is presented as an obstruction to naive asymptotics because multiples on the line overcontribute, but the ambient form remains nondegenerate.
The irrational ternary form with a rational isotropic line is used as an enemy case where multiples of the null vector contribute an anomalously large term.
The irrational form with a rational isotropic line is an enemy case for clean asymptotics since the restriction to that line is identically zero and contributes extra points.

The irrational form contains a rational null line, a collapsed q=0 direction whose multiples create excess counts.

examples.name: Degenerate restriction on a rational plane
examples.note: The speaker sketches an irrational ternary form with no obvious integral zero but whose restriction to a rational two-plane is singular, showing that primitive vectors alone do not remove all exceptional contributions and motivating the plane terms in the theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: for the rational plane example, the speaker restricts the form to the plane x=0, notes the restriction becomes degenerate, and reasons that this plane contributes on the order of the main term

passes.reason: The rational plane has a singular restricted quadratic form and is used to show primitive counting can still have excess contributions from degenerate subspaces.
